figures.gloss: The founder of sociology (1798-1857) who, after his transfiguring attachment to Clotilde de Vaux, built positivism into a full church: dogma, cult, and calendar, with himself as Grand-Pretre de l'Humanite. His Systeme de politique positive (1851-54) and Catechisme positiviste (1852) legislate worship for a post-theological age.
figures.facet: He is the rationalist who concluded that reason alone cannot bind a society — and so engineered a religion the way one engineers an institution, complete and top-down, from within unbelief.
figures.provenance: Systeme de politique positive (1851-54); Catechisme positiviste (1852); Mary Pickering's three-volume intellectual biography of Comte.

figures.gloss: Humanity itself as the supreme being: the continuous whole of all who have served the species, in which the dead outnumber and govern the living. Immortality is 'subjective' — to be incorporated after death into the Great Being through the grateful memory of humankind.
figures.facet: A deity with a membership criterion: only those who served Humanity enter it, making worship, ethics, and historiography the same act.
figures.provenance: Systeme de politique positive, vol. II; Catechisme positiviste; discussed in T.R. Wright, 'The Religion of Humanity' (1986).

figures.gloss: Comte's 1849 calendar of thirteen 28-day months named for benefactors of Humanity — Moses, Homer, Aristotle, Archimedes, Caesar, Saint Paul, Charlemagne, Dante, Gutenberg, Shakespeare, Descartes, Frederick, Bichat — with each day commemorating a further worthy: a complete sanctoral cycle of secular saints, closing with the Day of All the Dead.
figures.facet: It transposes the Catholic calendar of saints onto the history of civilization, making the veneration of the dead a precise, dated liturgy of human progress.
figures.provenance: Calendrier positiviste (1849); analyzed in T.R. Wright and in Andrew Wernick, 'Auguste Comte and the Religion of Humanity' (2001).

figures.gloss: Clotilde de Vaux (1815-1846), whose death after Comte's 'year without parallel' made her the cult's angelic patroness; Comte prayed before her relics thrice daily and visited her tomb weekly. In the Systeme he crowned the cult with the 'Utopia of the Virgin-Mother,' and positivist iconography personifies Humanity as a woman of thirty bearing her son.
figures.facet: The feminine as moral providence in a Godless system: the tradition rebuilds the Madonna out of a real, dated woman, grief formalized into dogma.
figures.provenance: Systeme de politique positive, vol. IV; Comte's 'Confessions' and correspondence with Clotilde de Vaux; Mary Pickering on the 'annee sans pareille'.

figures.gloss: Sociolatry's life-liturgy: Presentation, Initiation, Admission, Destination, Marriage, Maturity, Retirement, Transformation (death), and — seven years after death, if judged worthy — Incorporation into the Great Being. Every stage of a civic life is consecrated to Humanity.
figures.facet: The sacrament's verdict is deferred and social: sanctity is not conferred at the font but adjudicated by posterity, seven years after the grave.
figures.provenance: Catechisme positiviste (1852); practice documented in the Brazilian Positivist Church; T.R. Wright, 'The Religion of Humanity'.

figures.gloss: The Igreja Positivista do Brasil (founded 1881 by Miguel Lemos and Raimundo Teixeira Mendes) built the Templo da Humanidade in Rio de Janeiro (inaugurated 1897, damaged by roof collapse in 2009, under restoration), and Brazilian positivists later founded the Chapelle de l'Humanite on rue Payenne in Paris. Teixeira Mendes put Comte's motto on the new republic's flag in 1889: 'Ordem e Progresso.'
figures.facet: The faith's most durable trace is on a national flag: a church that barely survives as congregation persists as state iconography, its creed flown daily by millions who no longer read it.
figures.provenance: Igreja Positivista do Brasil records; the 19 Nov 1889 flag decree (Teixeira Mendes's design, from Comte's formula 'love as principle, order as basis, progress as goal'); Wernick (2001).

figures.gloss: Comte's designated executor (1823-1903), who led orthodox Parisian positivism through the half-century after the founder's 1857 death, holding the Religion of Humanity together as an organized cult. He became the first holder of the Collège de France chair in the history of science in 1892.
figures.facet: The second pontificate: institutional continuity is itself the achievement here, not new doctrine — Laffitte's work was to keep Comte's church a going concern rather than to extend it.
figures.provenance: T.R. Wright, 'The Religion of Humanity' (1986); Mary Pickering's three-volume biography of Comte, on Laffitte's succession.

figures.gloss: Oxford don turned Comtean priest (1818-1899) who founded the London positivist community (Chapel Street, 1870; later the Church of Humanity), performing Comte's sacraments and reciting his calendar in English — the tradition's second national body after Paris.
figures.facet: Positivism crosses the Channel as a working liturgy, not a philosophy department import: Congreve transplanted the whole cult apparatus — sacraments, calendar, priesthood — intact into English.
figures.provenance: T.R. Wright, 'The Religion of Humanity' (1986), on the English positivist congregations; Andrew Wernick, 'Auguste Comte and the Religion of Humanity' (2001).

figures.gloss: Vice-director then head of the Igreja Positivista do Brasil (1855-1927), co-founder of the Rio apostolate with Miguel Lemos, and designer of the 1889 'Ordem e Progresso' flag inscribed on Brazil's new republican banner following Comte's motto.
figures.facet: The movement's single most consequential member by any secular measure: a church that today counts its congregation in the dozens left its creed flying daily over 200 million people who have never read the Catéchisme.
figures.provenance: Igreja Positivista do Brasil records; the 19 November 1889 flag decree; Wernick (2001).

figures.gloss: Comte's final triad of worship, set out in the Synthèse subjective (1856): Humanity as the Great Being, the Earth as the Great Fetish, and Space as the Great Medium — the dead cosmos re-consecrated as the ground and dwelling of the Great Being.
figures.facet: Comte's late system completes what the Grand Être alone leaves unfinished: a full cosmology of worship, not a single deity, in which even inert Earth and Space are drawn into the cult as objects of veneration.
figures.provenance: Auguste Comte, Synthèse subjective (1856); T.R. Wright (1986); Wernick (2001).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: the self-appointed High Priest and the collective deity he defined — founder and godhead in one act of system-building.
Documented transposition: Comte deliberately rebuilt Marian devotion — Madonna iconography, the Virgin-Mother utopia, a female moral providence — around Clotilde de Vaux and personified Humanity.
Comte modeled sociolatry's nine social sacraments explicitly on the Catholic sacramental system — the borrowing Huxley skewered as 'Catholicism minus Christianity' (1869).
Mirror-image exits from Christianity: Deism keeps the Creator and discards the church; Comte discards the Creator and keeps the church, transferring worship to Humanity itself.

Descendant-class seam: Comte coined 'altruism', and his positivist churches in England pioneered the godless congregational format — ethics, hymns, festivals without theology — that Ethical Culture (1876) and organized humanism inherited.
Both make humanity itself the ultimate object of moral devotion — but Comte builds it into a deity with rites, where secular humanism keeps it a principle and refuses the altar.
Intra-tradition: the calendar is the Great Being made legible — the roll of the incorporated dead arranged into the worshipping year.
New calendars built to replace the inherited one, each a symbolic reordering of time meant to mark and enact a new dispensation.

Two invented civic liturgies replacing church ritual with a rationalist state religion — Robespierre's one-day festival for the Supreme Being anticipates Comte's fuller positivist calendar of Great Beings.
Comte named Laffitte his successor as director of positivism, giving the Paris cult its second pontificate.
Congreve exported Comte's calendar and sacraments to England, founding the London Church of Humanity as positivism's second national body.
Teixeira Mendes co-founded the Rio apostolate with Miguel Lemos and designed the flag motto associated with the Templo da Humanidade's cult.
Teixeira Mendes's 'Ordem e Progresso' flag motto distills Comte's formula for the cult of the Great Being into state iconography.
The Great Being is the senior term of Comte's completed trinity; the two entries document the same doctrinal system at different scales.
A structural three-in-one completing worship in each system, though positivism's triad is cosmological and social (Humanity, Earth, Space) rather than three persons of one Godhead.

The Positivist Calendar's first month venerates Moses as a secular benefactor of Humanity, one name among thirteen; Samaritanism carries the same historical-legendary person as the Sole Prophet of God — worship with the revelation surgically removed.

dossier.essence: The Religion of Humanity is the church Auguste Comte built out of positivist philosophy, announced in 1848 and legislated in detail in his Système de politique positive (1851-54) and Catéchisme positiviste (1852), with Comte himself as High Priest of Humanity. Its object of worship is the Grand Être — Humanity itself, the continuous whole of all who have served the species. Congregations flourished briefly in France, Britain, and above all Brazil, where the Igreja Positivista do Brasil (1881) put Comte's motto 'Ordem e Progresso' on the republic's flag in 1889 and maintains the Templo da Humanidade in Rio de Janeiro.
dossier.cosmos: The worldview is strictly positivist: knowledge passes through Comte's law of three stages — theological, metaphysical, positive — and in the final stage reality is known only through science, with no gods, no supernatural order, and no questions asked beyond phenomena and their laws. What replaces the absolute is the Great Being: the dead, who outnumber and govern the living, constitute Humanity's real substance. Immortality is 'subjective' — the worthy are incorporated after death into the Great Being through the grateful, organized memory of humankind; there is no other afterlife.
dossier.practice: Comte prescribed a complete cult: nine social sacraments from Presentation at birth to Incorporation seven years after death, a Positivist Calendar of thirteen 28-day months named for benefactors of Humanity with a secular saint for every day, daily private prayer addressed to one's personal angels (idealized women, on the pattern of Comte's own devotion to Clotilde de Vaux), and a salaried priesthood of Humanity charged with education and moral direction. The Brazilian church practiced the system most fully; in Britain the movement ran positivist churches whose format — ethics, hymns, and festivals without theology — fed later organized humanism.
dossier.canon: Comte's Système de politique positive (1851-54) and Catéchisme positiviste (1852; English as 'The Catechism of Positive Religion,' trans. Richard Congreve, 1858), with the Calendrier positiviste (1849) as its liturgical instrument.
dossier.reflects: The impulse to keep religion's binding and consoling machinery after belief in gods has gone — worship deliberately engineered, from within unbelief, as social technology.
sources: Auguste Comte, 'The Catechism of Positive Religion', trans. Richard Congreve (1858)
T.R. Wright, 'The Religion of Humanity: The Impact of Comtean Positivism on Victorian Britain' (1986)
Andrew Wernick, 'Auguste Comte and the Religion of Humanity: The Post-Theistic Program of French Social Theory' (2001)
Mary Pickering, 'Auguste Comte: An Intellectual Biography', Vol. 1 (1993)
